annotations.label: Algebraically closed field

anchor.snippet: a field F is algebraically closed if every non-constant polynomial with coefficients in F has a root in F

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `IsAlgClosed` by polynomial splitting and proves the root formulation as `IsAlgClosed.exists_root`.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem formulation

anchor.snippet: a field is algebraically closed if the fundamental theorem of algebra holds for it

annotations.note: Mathlib has the root-existence property for algebraically closed fields, but not this phrasing as a separate fundamental-theorem equivalence for an arbitrary field.

annotations.label: Real numbers not algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: the field of real numbers is not algebraically closed because the polynomial

annotations.note: Mathlib has semireal obstruction infrastructure implying negative one is not a sum of squares in ordered contexts, but I found no direct `¬ IsAlgClosed ℝ` declaration.

annotations.label: Complex numbers algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: the field of complex numbers is algebraically closed

annotations.note: `Complex.isAlgClosed` is the Mathlib instance that the complex numbers are algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure exists

anchor.snippet: Every field

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs `AlgebraicClosure k` and provides algebraic and algebraically closed instances for it.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure

anchor.snippet: the roots in

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosure F E` formalizes an algebraic closure as an algebraic algebra over `F` that is algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure uniqueness

anchor.snippet: Given two algebraic closures of

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosure.equiv` gives an algebra equivalence between two algebraic closures over the same base field.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed fields in class hierarchy

anchor.snippet: Algebraically closed fields appear in the following chain of class inclusions

annotations.note: Mathlib records some hierarchy consequences such as algebraically closed fields being perfect, but I found no formalized chain matching the article statement.

annotations.label: Real numbers example

anchor.snippet: the field of real numbers is not algebraically closed, because the polynomial equation

annotations.note: The semireal theorem gives related obstruction infrastructure for ordered fields, but I found no direct theorem stating that `ℝ` is not algebraically closed.

annotations.label: No real subfield is algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: no subfield of the real field is algebraically closed

annotations.note: Mathlib has semireal obstruction lemmas relevant to subfields of ordered fields, but I found no direct declaration that every subfield of `ℝ` is not algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Rational numbers not algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: the field of rational numbers is not algebraically closed

annotations.note: Mathlib has explicit irreducible rational polynomials of degree greater than one, but I found no direct `¬ IsAlgClosed ℚ` theorem.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra

anchor.snippet: the fundamental theorem of algebra states that the field of complex numbers is algebraically closed

annotations.note: `Complex.isAlgClosed` is Mathlib's formalization of the fundamental theorem of algebra in algebraically closed field form.

annotations.label: Algebraic numbers algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: Another example of an algebraically closed field is the field of (complex) algebraic numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib proves an abstract algebraic closure is algebraically closed, but I found no declaration identifying the complex algebraic numbers as that field.

annotations.label: No finite field is algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: No finite field F is algebraically closed

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosed.instInfinite` proves every algebraically closed field is infinite, which is equivalent to excluding algebraically closed finite fields.

annotations.label: Union of finite fields fixed characteristic

anchor.snippet: the union of all finite fields of a fixed characteristic p ( p prime) is an algebraically closed field

annotations.note: Mathlib has finite Galois fields as splitting fields of `X^(p^n)-X`, but I found no theorem forming their union and proving it algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure of finite field

anchor.snippet: which is, in fact, the algebraic closure of the field

annotations.note: Mathlib has general algebraic closures, but I found no declaration identifying the fixed-characteristic finite-field union with `AlgebraicClosure (ZMod p)`.

annotations.label: Complex rational function field not algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: of rational functions with complex coefficients is not closed

annotations.note: Mathlib has rational-function-field and minpoly infrastructure, but I found no direct theorem that `RatFunc ℂ` is not algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Square root rational function roots absent

anchor.snippet: for example, the polynomial

annotations.note: Mathlib has valuations on rational functions that can support such square-root obstructions, but I found no direct statement that the indicated polynomial has no root in `RatFunc ℂ`.

annotations.label: Algebraic closedness has equivalent assertions

anchor.snippet: the assertion " F is algebraically closed" is equivalent to other assertions

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes several equivalent-looking consequences of algebraic closedness, but I found no bundled theorem listing all article equivalences.

annotations.label: Irreducible polynomials have degree one

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if the only irreducible polynomials in the polynomial ring F [ x ] are those of degree one

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the algebraically-closed-to-linear-irreducible direction, but I found no exact iff theorem under this statement.

annotations.label: Linear polynomials irreducible

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: the polynomials of degree one are irreducible

annotations.note: `Polynomial.irreducible_of_degree_eq_one` states that a polynomial over a field with degree one is irreducible.

annotations.label: Irreducible polynomial over algebraically closed field is linear

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: If F is algebraically closed and p ( x ) is an irreducible polynomial of F [ x ]

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosed.degree_eq_one_of_irreducible` proves that irreducible polynomials over an algebraically closed field have degree one.

annotations.label: Non-algebraically closed field has irreducible polynomial of degree greater than one

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: if F is not algebraically closed, then there is some non-constant polynomial p ( x ) in F [ x ] without roots in F

annotations.note: Mathlib has irreducible-factor existence for positive-degree polynomials, but I found no direct contrapositive statement from `¬ IsAlgClosed` to a nonlinear irreducible polynomial.

annotations.label: Irreducible factor of rootless polynomial has no roots

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: Since p ( x ) has no roots in F , q ( x ) also has no roots in F

annotations.note: `Polynomial.IsRoot.dvd` gives the divisibility/root transfer whose contrapositive yields that a factor of a rootless polynomial has no root.

annotations.label: First degree polynomial has a root

anchor.section: The only irreducible polynomials are those of degree one
anchor.snippet: every first degree polynomial has one root in F

annotations.note: `Polynomial.exists_root_of_degree_eq_one` proves that every degree-one polynomial over a field has a root.

annotations.label: Polynomial splitting criterion

anchor.section: Every polynomial is a product of first degree polynomials
anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if every polynomial p ( x ) of degree n ≥ 1, with coefficients in F , splits into linear factors

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `IsAlgClosed` by the condition that every polynomial over the field splits.

annotations.label: Linear factor form

anchor.section: Every polynomial is a product of first degree polynomials
anchor.snippet: there are elements k , x 1 , x 2 , ..., x n of the field F such that p ( x ) = k ( x − x 1 )( x − x 2 ) ⋯ ( x − x n )

annotations.note: `Polynomial.splits_iff_exists_multiset` expresses splitting as a product of linear factors indexed by roots with a leading coefficient.

annotations.label: Root from splitting property

anchor.section: Every polynomial is a product of first degree polynomials
anchor.snippet: If F has this property, then clearly every non-constant polynomial in F [ x ] has some root in F

annotations.note: `Polynomial.Splits.exists_eval_eq_zero` proves that a nonconstant split polynomial has a root.

annotations.label: Factorization into irreducibles

anchor.section: Every polynomial is a product of first degree polynomials
anchor.snippet: for any field K , any polynomial in K [ x ] can be written as a product of irreducible polynomials

annotations.note: `Polynomial.uniqueFactorizationMonoid` gives the unique-factorization structure on polynomial rings over fields, implying factorization into irreducibles.

annotations.label: Prime degree root criterion

anchor.section: Polynomials of prime degree have roots
anchor.snippet: If every polynomial over F of prime degree has a root in F , then every non-constant polynomial has a root in F

annotations.note: Mathlib has irreducible-factor infrastructure for positive-degree polynomials, but I found no theorem reducing root existence to prime-degree polynomials.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed iff prime degree roots

anchor.section: Polynomials of prime degree have roots
anchor.snippet: a field is algebraically closed if and only if every polynomial over F of prime degree has a root in F

annotations.note: Mathlib proves root existence for all positive-degree polynomials over algebraically closed fields, but I found no iff using only prime degrees.

annotations.label: No proper algebraic extension criterion

anchor.section: The field has no proper algebraic extension
anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if it has no proper algebraic extension

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that an integral algebra over an algebraically closed field has bijective algebra map, but I found no exact iff packaged as no proper algebraic extensions.

annotations.label: Quotient by irreducible polynomial is algebraic extension

anchor.section: The field has no proper algebraic extension
anchor.snippet: the quotient of F [ x ] modulo the ideal generated by p ( x ) is an algebraic extension of F whose degree is equal to the degree of p ( x )

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the quotient by the span of a polynomial has finrank equal to its natDegree, with field structure for irreducible polynomials via `AdjoinRoot.instField`.

annotations.label: Proper algebraic extension contains element with nonlinear minimal polynomial

anchor.section: The field has no proper algebraic extension
anchor.snippet: if F has some proper algebraic extension K , then the minimal polynomial of an element in K \ F is irreducible and its degree is greater than 1

annotations.note: `minpoly.two_le_natDegree_iff` characterizes elements not coming from the base by minimal polynomial degree at least two, alongside the standard irreducibility of `minpoly`.

annotations.label: No proper finite extension criterion

anchor.section: The field has no proper finite extension
anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if it has no proper finite extension

annotations.note: Mathlib has the algebraically-closed-to-trivial-integral-extension direction and finite extensions are algebraic, but I found no exact finite-extension iff theorem.

annotations.label: Finite extensions algebraic

anchor.section: The field has no proper finite extension
anchor.snippet: Finite extensions are necessarily algebraic

annotations.note: `Algebra.IsAlgebraic.of_finite` proves that a finite algebra extension is algebraic.

annotations.label: Eigenvector criterion

anchor.section: Every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector
anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if, for each natural number n , every linear map from F n into itself has some eigenvector

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the algebraically-closed-to-eigenvalue direction for finite-dimensional nontrivial spaces, but I found no converse iff criterion.

annotations.label: Eigenvector iff characteristic polynomial has root

anchor.section: Every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector
anchor.snippet: An endomorphism of F n has an eigenvector if and only if its characteristic polynomial has some root

annotations.note: `Module.End.hasEigenvalue_iff_isRoot_charpoly` formalizes the equivalence between eigenvalues and roots of the characteristic polynomial.

annotations.label: Algebraic closedness gives eigenvectors

anchor.section: Every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector
anchor.snippet: when F is algebraically closed, every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector

annotations.note: `Module.End.exists_eigenvalue` proves that an endomorphism over an algebraically closed field on a nontrivial finite-dimensional space has an eigenvalue, yielding an eigenvector by existing eigenspace lemmas.

annotations.label: Monic rescaling preserves roots

anchor.section: Every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector
anchor.snippet: Dividing by its leading coefficient, we get another polynomial q ( x ) which has roots if and only if p ( x ) has roots

annotations.note: `Polynomial.roots_C_mul` shows multiplication by a nonzero scalar does not change roots, which is the substance of monic rescaling preserving roots.

annotations.label: Companion matrix characteristic polynomial

anchor.section: Every endomorphism of F n has some eigenvector
anchor.snippet: q ( x ) is the characteristic polynomial of the n×n companion matrix

annotations.note: I found characteristic-polynomial infrastructure but no verified companion-matrix declaration with this characteristic-polynomial theorem.

annotations.label: Partial fractions criterion

anchor.snippet: The field F is algebraically closed if and only if every rational function in one variable x , with coefficients in F , can be written as the sum of a polynomial function with rational functions of the form a /( x − b ) n

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general partial-fraction decomposition theorem, but I found no iff criterion characterizing algebraically closed fields by linear-denominator decompositions.

annotations.label: Partial fractions over algebraically closed fields

anchor.snippet: If F is algebraically closed then, since the irreducible polynomials in F [ x ] are all of degree 1, the property stated above holds by the theorem on partial fraction decomposition

annotations.note: Mathlib has the needed partial-fraction theorem and the linearity of irreducibles over algebraically closed fields, but I found no combined statement for this property.

annotations.label: Partial fractions force irreducibles linear

anchor.snippet: suppose that the property stated above holds for the field F

annotations.note: The partial-fraction machinery is present, but I found no theorem deriving linearity of irreducibles from the stated rational-function decomposition property.

annotations.label: Denominator product of linear polynomials

anchor.snippet: can be written as a quotient of two polynomials in which the denominator is a product of first degree polynomials

annotations.note: `Polynomial.splits_iff_exists_multiset` formalizes products of linear factors, but I found no exact denominator-normalization theorem in the partial-fractions context.

annotations.label: Relatively prime polynomials have no common root

anchor.snippet: For any field F , if two polynomials p ( x ), q ( x ) ∈ F [ x ] are relatively prime then they do not have a common root

annotations.note: `Polynomial.aeval_ne_zero_of_isCoprime` proves that coprime polynomials cannot vanish at the same element.

annotations.label: Common-root converse characterizes algebraically closed fields

anchor.snippet: The fields for which the reverse implication holds

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the coprime/common-root criterion over an algebraically closed field, but I found no theorem that the converse property characterizes algebraic closedness.

annotations.label: Non-coprime polynomials over algebraically closed field have common root

anchor.snippet: If the field F is algebraically closed, let p ( x ) and q ( x ) be two polynomials which are not relatively prime

annotations.note: `Polynomial.isCoprime_iff_aeval_ne_zero_of_isAlgClosed` gives the equivalence over algebraically closed fields, including the common-root consequence for non-coprime polynomials.

annotations.label: Non-algebraically closed counterexample to common-root converse

anchor.snippet: If F is not algebraically closed, let p ( x ) be a polynomial whose degree is at least 1 without roots

annotations.note: Mathlib has a constructor from root existence for monic irreducibles to algebraic closedness, but I found no direct counterexample theorem for the failed common-root converse.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed fields contain roots of unity

anchor.snippet: If F is an algebraically closed field and n is a natural number, then F contains all n th roots of unity

annotations.note: `IsSepClosed.hasEnoughRootsOfUnity`, together with the instance from algebraically closed to separably closed, gives roots-of-unity existence under the usual nonzero-characteristic hypothesis for `n`.

annotations.label: Cyclotomic extension

anchor.snippet: A field extension that is contained in an extension generated by the roots of unity is a cyclotomic extension

annotations.note: `IsCyclotomicExtension` formalizes cyclotomic extensions using primitive roots of unity and generation by those roots.

annotations.label: Cyclotomic closure

anchor.snippet: the extension of a field generated by all roots of unity is sometimes called its cyclotomic closure

annotations.note: Mathlib has cyclotomic-extension generation criteria, but I found no separate `CyclotomicClosure` definition.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed fields cyclotomically closed

anchor.snippet: Thus algebraically closed fields are cyclotomically closed

annotations.note: Mathlib proves separably closed fields give cyclotomic extensions in the formal `IsCyclotomicExtension` sense, but I found no statement using a `cyclotomically closed` predicate.

annotations.label: Cyclotomically closed converse false

anchor.snippet: The converse is not true

annotations.note: I found no verified Mathlib counterexample showing a cyclotomically closed field that is not algebraically closed.

annotations.label: Binomial splitting insufficient

anchor.snippet: Even assuming that every polynomial of the form x n − a splits into linear factors is not enough to assure that the field is algebraically closed

annotations.note: I found no verified Mathlib theorem or counterexample formalizing that splitting all binomials `X^n - a` is insufficient for algebraic closedness.

annotations.label: First-order transfer for same characteristic

anchor.snippet: If a proposition which can be expressed in the language of first-order logic is true for an algebraically closed field, then it is true for every algebraically closed field with the same characteristic

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Field.ACF_isComplete` formalizes completeness of the theory of algebraically closed fields of fixed characteristic, giving the first-order transfer principle.

annotations.label: Lefschetz principle finite characteristic bound

anchor.snippet: if such a proposition is valid for an algebraically closed field with characteristic 0

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Field.ACF_zero_realize_iff_finite_ACF_prime_not_realize` formalizes the all-but-finitely-many-primes transfer from characteristic zero algebraically closed fields.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed extension exists

anchor.snippet: Every field F has some extension which is algebraically closed

annotations.note: `AlgebraicClosure k` provides an algebraically closed field extension of any field `k`.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed extension

anchor.snippet: Such an extension is called an algebraically closed extension

annotations.note: Mathlib represents an algebraically closed extension by an algebra structure whose target has an `IsAlgClosed` instance, but I found no separate named definition `AlgebraicallyClosedExtension`.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure existence and uniqueness

anchor.snippet: Among all such extensions there is one and only one

annotations.note: Existence is provided by `AlgebraicClosure`, and `IsAlgClosure.equiv` gives uniqueness up to algebra equivalence.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure of a field

anchor.snippet: it is called the algebraic closure of F

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosure F E` is the Mathlib predicate for `E` being an algebraic closure of `F`.

annotations.label: Quantifier elimination for algebraically closed fields

anchor.snippet: The theory of algebraically closed fields has quantifier elimination

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the first-order theory `ACF` and completeness results for it, but I found no quantifier-elimination theorem for algebraically closed fields.

annotations.label: No algebraically closed finite field

anchor.snippet: There is no algebraically closed finite field

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosed.instInfinite` directly rules out finite algebraically closed fields by providing an infinite instance for any algebraically closed field.
